title: Wireshark OSI Detective
description: Analyze a provided packet capture snippet and identify layers and headers.
problemStatement:
## Packet Capture Analysis

Below is a simplified representation of a single captured frame from Wireshark (a packet sniffer). Analyze it and answer the questions.

```text
Frame 1: 74 bytes on wire (592 bits)
Ethernet II, Src: Apple_a1:b2:c3 (a1:b2:c3:d4:e5:f6), Dst: Cisco_11:22:33 (11:22:33:44:55:66)
Internet Protocol Version 4, Src: 192.168.1.105, Dst: 142.250.185.46
Transmission Control Protocol, Src Port: 52134, Dst Port: 443, Seq: 1, Ack: 1, Len: 20
Transport Layer Security (TLSv1.2)
```

## Questions

1. What is the destination **MAC** address of this frame? (Layer 2)
2. What is the source **IP** address? (Layer 3)
3. What is the **Transport Layer protocol** and **destination port**? (Layer 4)
4. Based on the port and the presence of TLS, what **Layer 7 application** is likely being used?
5. At which layer would you look to see if the data was fragmented across multiple packets?
6. If we wanted to see the actual HTTP `GET` request inside this packet, why can't we see it directly? (Hint: Think Layer 6).
hints: MAC addresses are in the Ethernet II section.
TLS encrypts the payload, hiding Layer 7 data from sniffers.
expectedOutput:
1. Destination MAC: 11:22:33:44:55:66 (Cisco router).
2. Source IP: 192.168.1.105.
3. Transport Protocol: TCP. Destination Port: 443.
4. Application: HTTPS (Secure Web Browsing).
5. Fragmentation information is in the IP Header (Layer 3).
6. Because TLS (Layer 6) has encrypted the application data (Layer 7). Only the encrypted bytes are visible.
